summary: Dan Luu argues that while HN comments are mostly wrong and often mean-spirited, it remains the best public technical forum available because well-informed comments, though rare, tend to rise to the top. He curates dozens of excellent HN comments across topics like software engineering, business operations, and tech industry dynamics to demonstrate the hidden value buried in comment threads. The post explores what makes HN's comment quality superior to other forums, attributing it to careful ranking algorithms that penalize flamebait and heavily weight user flags, though the ultimate cause may simply be network effects and concentration of expertise.
key_points: HN comments are often wrong and mean-spirited, but well-informed comments typically float to top when they exist
By volume, there are more interesting technical insights in HN comments than in posted links
HN's ranking system penalizes high-comment-count flamebait and weights user flags heavily, unlike Reddit's simple vote cancellation
Great HN comments tend to disappear over time unlike blog posts, representing lost valuable content
HN's success may be due to network effects and concentration of expertise rather than replicable design choices
money_quotes: On topics I'm familiar with, while it's rare that a thread will have even a single comment that's well-informed, when those comments appear, they usually float to the top.

Most people realize by their 30s that prestige is a sucker's game; it's a way of inducing people to do things that aren't much fun and they wouldn't really want to do on their own, by lauding them with accolades from people they don't really care about.
Even when the site is civil, it's still committed to grind away any joy you take either in accomplishing something near or even in just sharing something interesting you learned.

Blockchain is the world's worst database, created entirely to maintain the reputations of venture capital firms who injected hundreds of millions of dollars into a technology whose core defining insight was 'You can improve on a Ponzi scam by making it self-organizing and distributed.'
My favorite developers ever all do badly in your typical stringent Silicon Valley interview. They work slower, do more thinking, and consider every line of code they write technical debt.
key_insight: HN's value lies not in its average comment quality but in its unique ability to surface rare expert insights to the top, though this valuable knowledge gets lost over time unlike blog posts.
